title: Foster doe management in meat rabbits
subtitle: Moving kits between does when a litter is too large or the doe rejects it.
excerpt: A doe with twelve or more kits will struggle to raise all of them on her own, the rear kits in a large litter don't always reach the teats. Moving surplus kits to a doe with a smaller litter evens the load and keeps more kits alive. Here's when to foster, how to mask the kits' scent, and how to check the foster doe accepts them.
sourceNotes: Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living, rabbit chapter. University of Wisconsin Extension, Rabbit Production guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A female rabbit kept for breeding; she carries and raises the kits.
A newborn or very young rabbit.

aliases: moving rabbit kits to another doe

content.text: once or twice a day for five to ten minutes each time. Her milk production is not infinitely scalable; a litter of over ten kits in a colony system often has one or two small, thin 'peanut' kits that are not getting adequate nutrition. Foster management, identifying the problem and moving kits before they're too weak, keeps more kits to weaning weight.
Litter size exceeds ten healthy kits and the doe is showing signs of weight loss or poor condition by day five
One or more kits are noticeably smaller than their litter-mates and feel cold to the touch
A doe has kindled but rejected the litter, she hasn't pulled fur, hasn't fed them, and the kits are cold and scattered
A doe dies shortly after kindling

The ideal foster doe has kindled within two to three days of the original doe, a size match between the foster kits and the doe's own kits makes integration easier. A doe with a very small litter (two or three kits) is a better foster candidate than one with eight already. Don't foster more than two kits per move.

Rabbits identify their own kits partly by nest scent. Before transferring kits, rub them with a small amount of nest fur from the foster doe's nest, pull out a few loose strands and roll the foster kits in them. This reduces the probability of the doe pulling them out and ignoring them.
Move the foster doe away from the nest box while you work. Place the kits to transfer into the centre of the nest, under the existing kits, not on top.
Cover the nest with the nest fur and replace the doe. Allow 30 minutes before checking.
Check the transferred kits for full bellies at the next feeding time (12 hours). A full belly is soft and slightly rounded; an empty belly is wrinkled and flat.

Kits found scattered in the cage cold and apparently dead may still be revivable if the gut is not yet empty and they haven't been cold for more than a few hours. Warm them slowly in your hands or against your body and check for breathing. A warm kit that recovers and gains belly fullness after fostering usually does well. One that recovers but stays thin and cold-feeling despite feeding is likely injured or too small to compete.
